These fixes ensure proper functionality of accessibility features such as Explore by Touch.

entries.title: Custom view accessibility support on Android TV
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on implementing accessibility support for custom views in Android TV applications. It emphasizes the importance of providing accessibility services with information about custom views, especially when dealing with OpenGL or Canvas, to ensure proper function with Talkback and Switch Access. The document offers solutions and best practices to address common accessibility issues.

entries.title: Adopt system caption settings
entries.summary: This guide now clarifies that it demonstrates how an app can obtain and apply the system-provided caption style on Android TV. The introductory text and some phrasing were updated for clarity, and minor formatting changes were made. The guide continues to explain how to get the CaptioningManager and handle caption style changes.

entries.title: Support TalkBack in TV apps
entries.summary: The documentation for supporting TalkBack in TV apps has been updated. The changes focus on improving clarity and formatting, while providing more detail on how to make views focusable, modify action descriptions, and implement slider support.

entries.title: TalkBack evaluation examples for TV apps
entries.summary: The document has been updated to improve readability and clarity. Changes include formatting adjustments, minor wording refinements, and consistent use of bold formatting. These changes improve the guidance on evaluating TV apps for TalkBack accessibility, such as adding more instructions on how to evaluate the app, as well as fixing a minor formatting issue.

entries.title: Help users find your content on Android TV
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of how users can discover content on Android TV. The changes primarily involve minor text adjustments, such as improved formatting, HTML to Markdown conversion, and updated links to related documentation, but the core concepts regarding content recommendation and searchability remain the same.

entries.title: Audio program attributes
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to improve readability and formatting of the table describing audio program attributes.  The table's markdown was also corrected.

entries.title: Game program attributes
entries.summary: The documentation for game program attributes has been updated to improve readability and formatting.  The changes include minor text revisions and a table reformatting for better presentation.

entries.title: Watch Next guidelines for app developers
entries.summary: The documentation for Android TV's Watch Next channel guidelines has been updated with minor clarifications and formatting adjustments.  Key changes include specifying that content should be added to the Watch Next channel immediately when the user exits the app or pauses playback for more than 5 minutes, and that the Content ID attribute must be set for every video program to match the internal ID in the Media Actions feed.

entries.title: Watch Next guidelines for TV providers
entries.summary: This document provides guidelines for TV providers regarding the Watch Next channel. The changes enhance clarity and formatting, including improvements to the structure, formatting, and wording, such as the addition of markdown formatting for clarity, and improved sentence structure for better readability. These changes ensure the guidelines are clear and easy to understand for developers, leading to a better user experience on Android TV.

entries.title: Preview videos
entries.summary: This document describes the process of creating and rendering preview videos for Android TV applications. The changes include improvements in formatting, links to references, and clarifications of existing content. These updates provide better clarity and a more user-friendly experience for developers creating preview videos.

entries.title: Channels on the home screen
entries.summary: This document introduces how to add and manage recommendation channels and programs on the Android TV home screen. The changes include improvements in formatting, updated links, and clarification of existing concepts. Several code examples were also updated.

entries.title: Recommend content on the home screen
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify how Android TV displays recommendations using channels and programs. It also provides more detail on the default channel and the Watch Next channel, and compatibility considerations for different Android versions, including a note about the temporary nature of forward-compatible behavior.

entries.title: Video program attributes
entries.summary: The table displaying video program attributes has been reformatted for improved readability. The markdown table format was adjusted to improve clarity, with no functional changes to the content.

entries.title: Add programs to the Watch Next channel
entries.summary: The documentation for adding programs to the Watch Next channel has been updated. The changes include more descriptive text about the channel, including details about the types of programs that can be added and the process for certification on Google TV. The formatting has also been slightly updated for better readability.

entries.title: Watch Next attributes
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to reflect improvements in formatting and clarity. The text was updated to use a note block and the table's formatting was updated.

entries.title: Build TV games
entries.summary: The documentation for Android TV games has been updated to provide more accurate links for Android developers. It updates hyperlinks for methods and attributes, and fixes minor formatting issues.

entries.title: Best practices to drive engagement on Google TV
entries.summary: This document provides best practices for developing apps for Google TV, covering key areas like content discovery, voice integration, and user acquisition.  It suggests that developers support Google Cast, media sessions, media actions feeds, Watch Next, account linking, entitlement sync, voice casting, Cast Connect, Google Play Billing, and frictionless subscriptions to improve the user experience on Google TV. Also added formatting and markdown improvements.

entries.title: AndroidX TV libraries
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to emphasize the use of Android architecture components like ViewModel and Room, along with Compose, to share application logic between form factors when building Android TV apps. This promotes modern UI development and efficient code reuse.

entries.title: Multitasking on TV
entries.summary: The documentation updates incorporate changes to formatting, and use of `[!NOTE]` for notes. Additionally, there are minor adjustments to code examples and clarifications regarding the `preferKeepClear` attribute and the usage of picture-in-picture mode. These changes improve readability and clarity.

entries.title: TV navigation
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of navigation on Android TV devices. The changes include formatting improvements, updates to the wording, and minor clarifications to the existing content. The document also provides a better note about the remote controller and its features.

entries.title: On-screen keyboard
entries.summary: The documentation for on-screen keyboards on Android TV has been updated to reflect improvements and features in Gboard. Key changes include enhanced formatting for readability, updated descriptions, and clarified information about keyboard placement and alignment options. This ensures developers can easily implement and customize on-screen keyboards for their Android TV applications.

entries.title: Media Quality framework
entries.summary: The documentation for the `PictureProfile` object was updated. No substantive changes were made; the content was reworded for clarity.

entries.title: Ambient Mode
entries.summary: The documentation for Android TV's Ambient Mode has been updated. The changes include text formatting, the addition of a note block, and minor wording adjustments for clarity.

entries.title: Leanback UI toolkit libraries
entries.summary: The documentation updates reflect that the Leanback library is deprecated, with instructions to use Jetpack Compose for Android TV OS. Additionally, some minor formatting changes and clarifications are made throughout the document. It also includes an update on the limitation of the Leanback tab library and its theme supports.

entries.title: Migrate to Compose for TV
entries.summary: The documentation provides updated guidance on migrating from Leanback UI toolkit to Compose for Android TV. It emphasizes a gradual migration approach, leveraging dedicated Compose components, adapting data and state management, and testing throughout the process. The changes include improved formatting and clearer wording for better understanding.

entries.title: Use transport controls
entries.summary: This document has been significantly updated to reflect the deprecation of the Leanback library and the recommendation to use Jetpack Compose for Android TV OS instead. It also incorporates minor updates to improve clarity and formatting, including the use of more concise descriptions and updated links.

entries.title: Implement a media session
entries.summary: The documentation for media sessions has been updated to improve readability and clarity. The changes include formatting adjustments, such as adding line breaks and clarifying sentences for better understanding.

entries.title: Optimize memory usage
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on optimizing memory usage for Android TV apps, focusing on the specific challenges and constraints of TV devices, particularly those with low RAM. It highlights the potential problems caused by high memory consumption and defines memory targets for different device types.

entries.title: Apps
entries.summary: The documentation updates provide clearer explanations and improved formatting for app development on Wear OS. The changes enhance readability and clarify important concepts such as the use of Jetpack Compose and UX principles.

entries.title: Appear in Recents and app resume
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to improve clarity and formatting. Changes include minor rewording and improved formatting in the text, and improved code samples.

entries.title: Conserve power and battery
entries.summary: This document focuses on improving power efficiency in Wear OS apps. It emphasizes the importance of minimizing battery drain due to the smaller battery size of Wear OS devices and provides best practices to achieve this, including deferring tasks, utilizing always-on mode, and minimizing screen-on time and CPU usage.

entries.title: Test Bluetooth audio on emulators
entries.summary: This document explains how to test Bluetooth audio functionality on Wear OS emulators. It guides users on pairing with emulated phones and playing audio through emulated Bluetooth output devices, providing steps for setting up the environment using the Bumble project.

entries.title: Migrate from Material 2.5 to Material 3 in Compose for Wear OS
entries.summary: This guide provides instructions and considerations for migrating Android Wear OS applications built with Wear Compose Material 2.5 to the new Wear Compose Material 3 Jetpack library. The changes include adding a description, keywords, and updating a link to a snippet file in the navigation example.

entries.title: Discover devices on a network using Data Layer APIs
entries.summary: This document describes how to discover devices on a network using Data Layer APIs. The changes include improvements to the readability of the text, and improved formatting.

entries.title: Connect a watch to a phone
entries.summary: This document provides updated instructions and clarifies the process of connecting a Wear OS watch to a phone, focusing on emulator pairing and phone setup. It includes updated steps and UI references for using the Wear OS emulator pairing assistant and setting up a phone to connect with a Wear OS watch, and addresses details about pairing multiple devices.

entries.title: Create and run your first Wear OS app
entries.summary: This document provides a guide for creating a Wear OS app using a template in Android Studio. It also introduces best practices for Wear OS app development. The changes include improvements in text formatting, rewording, and updates to the links and notes.

entries.title: Debug a Wear OS app
entries.summary: This document provides instructions for debugging Wear OS applications. It outlines the debugging process using both the Android Emulator and physical devices, including setup and connection methods. The document also includes information on installing specific OS versions, testing watch-phone connections, and capturing screenshots and videos.

entries.title: Test on the Wear OS emulator
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on testing Wear OS applications using the Android Emulator, highlighting specialized features, testing capabilities, and troubleshooting tips specific to Wear OS development. It covers sensor simulation, input simulation, device pairing, and known issues.

entries.title: Capture Wear UI screens
entries.summary: The documentation for capturing Wear OS UI screens has been updated. Formatting, link, and wording improvements were made for clarity and consistency. Minor clarifications were added for using adb screencap and scrcpy for screen recordings, and companion app screenshots.

entries.title: Develop experiences for kids on Wear OS
entries.summary: This document provides guidelines for developing Wear OS apps and watch faces optimized for kids, emphasizing battery life, standalone app considerations, and the use of Watch Face Format. The changes include improved formatting, clarification of standalone mode behavior, and the introduction of a method for displaying legal information using short-links or QR codes when the device is in standalone mode. It also clarifies best practices for checking the standalone mode status before authorization requests.

entries.title: Principles of Wear OS development
entries.summary: This document provides principles for developing Wear OS applications. The changes include improvements in the text, formatting, and some additions. The content emphasizes designing for core tasks, optimizing for the wrist, and respecting user privacy, providing more detailed guidance on how to implement each principle.

entries.title: Tiles
entries.summary: The documentation has undergone minor formatting changes, including line breaks and spacing adjustments. Additionally, link formatting was updated. These changes do not introduce new technical information.

entries.title: Tile animations
entries.summary: The documentation for animations in Wear tiles was updated. Specifically, the Kotlin code examples for animations, slide transitions, and transformations were updated and the data type for onTileRequest and onTileResourcesRequest has been updated.

entries.title: Lifecycle and analytics events
entries.summary: The documentation for Wear OS Tiles lifecycle was updated. Specifically, the text 'Caution:' was updated to use a callout block for emphasis.

entries.title: Wear OS user interfaces
entries.summary: The documentation updates include cosmetic changes to improve readability, such as replacing markdown syntax and refining wording.  The note regarding Compose for Wear OS has been updated to use the appropriate markdown syntax, and image spacing has been fixed.

entries.title: Prepare your app for behavior changes in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: This document outlines changes in Wear OS 4, which is based on Android 13 (API level 33). It emphasizes the need to handle system behavior changes from Android 12 and 13. The document also provides information on how to improve app compatibility by targeting Android 13.

entries.title: Prepare your app for behavior changes in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: Wear OS 4 introduces new permission requirements for apps. Notably, apps now require notification runtime permission by default and background body sensors permission. Apps should also account for approximate location permission. This affects how apps request and handle user permissions for notifications, background sensor access, and location data.

entries.title: Prepare your app for behavior changes in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: Significant changes to app components and navigation are detailed, including stricter intent filter matching, modified root launcher activity behavior, stricter Android App Links verification, and the removal of the SYSTEM_ALERT_WINDOW UI. These changes impact how apps handle intents, lifecycle management, deep linking, and UI display.

entries.title: Prepare your app for behavior changes in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: This section covers modifications to power and data management, including the Restricted App Standby Bucket, App Hibernation, and Backup & Restore functionality. These changes impact app behavior in low-usage scenarios and introduce cloud backup capabilities.

entries.title: Explore features in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: This document outlines the new features introduced in Wear OS 4, including enhancements to watch faces, tiles, splash screens, and data transfer to new mobile devices. These updates provide developers with new tools and improve the user experience on Wear OS devices.

entries.title: Wear OS 5.1
entries.summary: This document introduces Wear OS 5.1, based on Android 15 (API level 35), and guides developers on preparing devices and emulators for testing, updating the target SDK, and exploring new features such as Credential Manager support and watch speaker playback.

entries.title: Test how your app handles behavior changes
entries.summary: This document outlines the changes and behavior changes in Wear OS 5, which is based on Android 14. It highlights changes affecting all apps and those specifically for apps targeting Android 14, including privacy dashboard support, watch face format requirements, backgrounding of always-on apps, foreground service type declarations for exercise-recording apps, device unlock duration, handling of draggable content, restrictions to implicit intents, and non-dismissible notifications.

entries.title: Explore features
entries.summary: This document outlines new features in Wear OS 5, including a grid-based app launcher, user control over media output devices, enhancements to Watch Face Format (including flavors, new complication types, weather data, and heart rate data), screenshot detection API, and updates to Health Services (detailed running metrics and debounced goals). These features improve the user experience and offer developers more tools for creating engaging and informative Wear OS apps.

entries.title: Update your app's target SDK version for Wear OS 5
entries.summary: This document provides instructions on how to update your Wear OS app to target Android 14 (API level 34). It guides developers through modifying the build.gradle or build.gradle.kts file to specify the target SDK version, highlighting the importance of handling system behavior changes for apps targeting Android 14 or higher.

entries.title: Test how your app handles behavior changes
entries.summary: This document outlines changes for Wear OS 6, based on Android 16 (API level 36). Key changes include a more consistent always-on display, default system font for tiles, more granular health permissions, and batched tile interaction events. These changes affect app behavior and require developers to update their apps for compatibility.

entries.title: Explore features
entries.summary: This document introduces the features of Wear OS 6, including support for Material 3 Expressive and enhancements to watch faces, along with continued support for features from previous versions. The update provides links to resources for migration and design principles, and it highlights improvements in layout and user experience, like expressive motion and multi-slot tile layouts, along with features in Watch Face Format v4 such as photo support and animated transitions.

entries.title: Prepare for Wear OS 6
entries.summary: This document provides instructions for setting up and testing Wear OS 6 emulators, and updating apps to target Wear OS 6. It includes updated instructions and notes on emulator setup, including downloading the latest Android Studio preview, and selecting the correct system image. It also adds a note about app publishing requirements on Google Play.

entries.title: Handle different watch shapes
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to reflect the current best practices for Wear OS development. It promotes the use of Jetpack Compose for UI development, provides updated links to the Wear OS UI Library and design guidelines, and clarifies the usage of BoxInsetLayout and WearableRecyclerView for supporting different watch shapes.  The code examples are updated with correct links and formatting.

entries.title: Customize watch face appearance using Watch Face Designer
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to rephrase the introduction and convert the existing note format to a more modern "NOTE" block. These changes improve readability and clarity.

entries.title: Advanced usage of Watch Face Designer
entries.summary: The documentation for advanced watch face design in Figma was updated to use the standard note block formatting for the note about folder representation in styles, changing the markdown syntax from a pipe-based table to a note block.

entries.title: Basics of using Watch Face Designer
entries.summary: The documentation for watch face designers was updated to replace a standard markdown note with an admonition block using the `> [!NOTE]` syntax. This change highlights the platform dependency of the user-provided photo feature, clarifying that it's only available on Wear OS 6 or higher.

entries.title: Export your watch face
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to use the `> [!NOTE]` callout syntax instead of bolded "Note:" text to highlight important information. This change improves the visual clarity and consistency of the documentation.

entries.title: Copyright (c) 2015 Calvin Metcalf
entries.summary: This document, which lists the open-source licenses used in the Watch Face Designer, has been updated. The changes primarily involve formatting improvements to the license text, like line breaks and spacing, to enhance readability. There are no substantial content changes.

entries.title: Publish from Watch Face Designer to Google Play
entries.summary: The documentation for publishing watch faces created with Watch Face Designer has been updated to use the modern "NOTE" and "CAUTION" callout blocks instead of bolded text for emphasis, and updated the images in the doc to reflect the latest UI changes. This improves readability and provides a more consistent look and feel with other Android documentation.

entries.title: Configure your phone app for Watch Face Push
entries.summary: This document introduces changes to the Watch Face Push library, focusing on providing guidance for developers on setting a watch face and handling different scenarios during the activation process. It also clarifies the use of the library and suggests alternatives like Watch Face Designer or Watch Face Studio if publishing to Google Play.

entries.title: Add complications to a watch face
entries.summary: This document introduces how to integrate and set up watch face complications on Wear OS utilizing the Watch Face Format. It outlines the available complication types and their fields, providing guidance on displaying data from various sources.

entries.title: Improve your watch face performance with hardware acceleration
entries.summary: The documentation for hardware acceleration in Wear OS watch faces was updated to remove a redundant introductory sentence and to correct a minor formatting error in the ADB command example by removing unnecessary backticks.

entries.title: Watch Face Format
entries.summary: The documentation for Wear OS watch face creation has been updated. The changes include an update to a help center article link and a formatting change for the "Note" section, using the `> [!NOTE]` syntax to emphasize it. This change provides enhanced clarity to the reader.

entries.title: Build and deploy a watch face
entries.summary: The documentation for building Wear OS watch faces using the Watch Face Format has been updated. The changes include improved formatting, clearer descriptions of the watch face structure, and better explanations of the build process using Android Studio and Gradle. Additionally, the documentation now emphasizes pre-submission checks for watch faces before submitting to the Google Play Store, including details on the Memory Footprint Evaluator tool.

entries.title: Debug a watch face
entries.summary: This document describes how to debug a watch face built with the Watch Face Format. The primary change is reformatting and minor clarifications regarding XML validation and runtime error identification using the XML validation tool and logcat, respectively. It explains how to check for valid Watch Face Format documents and how to identify runtime errors.

entries.title: Support personalization
entries.summary: The documentation introduces the concept of watch face personalization, explaining how to allow users to customize both the watch face's appearance and the information it displays. It highlights the use of `UserConfigurations` for appearance customization and Complications for information customization. It also emphasizes the role of the Watch Face Editor for user changes and the need to set the watch face as editable to enable the editor.

entries.title: Work profile contacts
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to include better formatting, more details on UX, and clarify IT admin behavior. It also includes examples and clarifications for enterprise contacts.

entries.title: Build a device policy controller
entries.summary: This document introduces changes related to the deprecation of custom DPC registration, and updates the documentation to reflect the latest guidance. It adds a caution about the deprecation of custom DPCs and provides updated links and formatting.

entries.title: Dedicated devices overview
entries.summary: The documentation introduces APIs for managing Android devices dedicated to a specific purpose. This guide is aimed at enterprise mobility management (EMM) developers and solution integrators.

entries.title: Dedicated devices cookbook
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to improve clarity and formatting. Changes include adjustments to the wording and code examples for better readability and a more consistent presentation.  These changes enhance the user experience by making the content easier to understand and follow.

entries.title: Lock task mode
entries.summary: This document introduces minor formatting and wording changes. It adds a more prominent 'Important' note to the 'Key Term' section. It also adds table formatting using markdown syntax and refactors various code snippets.

entries.title: Device control
entries.summary: The documentation was updated with minor formatting changes, link updates, and clarifications. The most notable change is the addition of a sentence on how to access the device's Hardware Property Manager service and a note on not all hardware properties are available on all devices.

entries.title: Network activity logging
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to include information on network logging, including the types of events logged (DNS lookups and network connections), availability, and user notifications. The updates improve clarity by reformatting text and adding emphasis using callouts.

entries.title: Networking and telephony
entries.summary: This document underwent several changes. The changes include updating the links to the Android developer documentation, updating the table of features, and modifying some wording and formatting.

entries.title: RCS Google Messages archival
entries.summary: This document describes changes to the RCS Messages Archival documentation. It includes updates to the solution overview, workflow, implementation details (including Google Messages MCM schema, notification to archival app, and archival app requirements), and testing procedures. The changes clarify existing information and improve readability, and add an important note regarding syncing of every message event.

entries.title: Security
entries.summary: This document introduces changes to the security documentation, primarily involving the addition of the > [!NOTE] and > [!WARNING] blocks to highlight important information and the adb command to force security logs for testing purposes. These changes aim to enhance the clarity and emphasis of critical security aspects within the documentation.

entries.title: Manage system updates
entries.summary: This document introduces changes related to system update management for Android devices. The updates include clarifications and refinements to existing content, improved code examples, and adjustments to table contents to accurately reflect the functionality available to device and profile owners. The changes aim to enhance clarity and provide more accurate information regarding system update policies, callbacks, and freeze periods, improving the developer's understanding of DPC capabilities.

entries.title: What's new for Android in the enterprise
entries.summary: The documentation underwent several formatting changes, including adding line breaks for readability and updating links. These changes improve the overall presentation and clarity of the document, making it easier to understand the new features for each Android version in an enterprise setting.

entries.title: What's new for enterprise in Android 10
entries.summary: This document outlines changes in Android 10 related to enterprise features. Key updates include improved provisioning tools for work profiles, device-ID attestation for work profiles, and enhancements for work profile management such as device-wide blocking of app installations from unknown sources.

entries.title: What's new in Android 7.0
entries.summary: This document outlines the new features and API changes in Android 7.0 (API level 25) related to Android enterprise functionality. These updates enhance security, device management, and user experience within a work profile environment.

entries.title: What's new in Android 7.0
entries.summary: Key changes include support for QR code provisioning, a work profile security challenge, the ability to disable app access, work mode toggling, always-on VPN configuration, improved contact integration, remote reboot capabilities, a location off switch, customized provisioning options, multiple Wi-Fi CA certificate support, customized lock screen messages, and work profile connection services.

entries.title: What's new in Android 7.0
entries.summary: These changes improve enterprise device management by providing administrators with more control over devices, enhancing security with features like work profile challenges and always-on VPN, and improving the user experience with features like contact integration and the work mode toggle. This enables more secure and manageable corporate environments.

entries.title: What's new in Android 8.0
entries.summary: Android 8.0 introduces significant enhancements for enterprise Android deployments. Key changes include the ability to create work profiles on fully managed devices, enabling better separation of work and personal data. Additionally, user affiliation features allow for more device-wide control and functionality based on organizational identity. Several security improvements are also included, such as enhanced password reset and network logging capabilities.

entries.title: What’s in Android 9 for enterprise apps
entries.summary: This document outlines the enterprise-focused changes in Android 9. Key updates include UI enhancements for work profiles, APIs for cross-profile app switching, and expanded lock task mode capabilities. These changes aim to improve user experience and control in managed device scenarios.

entries.title: get.md
entries.summary: This document provides instructions on how to obtain and set up Android 10 for testing and development. The changes primarily involve formatting updates for better readability, and minor clarifications within the existing content. No significant new information or functional changes are introduced.

entries.title: setup-sdk.md
entries.summary: The documentation provides instructions on how to set up the Android 10 SDK in Android Studio, including getting the latest Android Studio, installing the SDK, and updating the build configuration. The changes primarily involve formatting and minor wording adjustments for better readability, such as adding line breaks and clarifying instructions. These changes help developers easily set up their environment and test Android 10 features and behavior changes.

entries.title: get.md
entries.summary: This document provides updated instructions and links for obtaining Android 12, including flashing, emulators, GSIs, and Android TV. The changes primarily refine the wording, improve clarity, and update links related to Android 12 resources. No significant feature changes were made.

entries.title: qrscanner.md
entries.summary: This document describes the Android 13 feature that adds a dedicated QR code scanner accessible from the Quick Settings tile to facilitate UPI payment flows. The changes include adding images, clarifying the behavior of the disambiguation dialog when multiple UPI apps are installed, and explaining how payment apps can identify the source of the QR code (live camera or static image) using intent extras.

entries.title: get.md
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to reflect minor formatting adjustments, improved clarity, and updated links for obtaining and setting up Android 13, including Pixel devices, emulators, and GSIs. These changes provide more accurate and accessible instructions for developers to test and utilize Android 13 features.

entries.title: setup-sdk.md
entries.summary: The documentation for setting up the Android 13 SDK has been updated. The changes include formatting adjustments for better readability, and minor clarifications without altering the core instructions.

entries.title: data-safety.md
entries.summary: Android 14 enhances user privacy by increasing the visibility of data safety information declared in the Play Console. This includes displaying data sharing practices in permission dialogs for location permissions if the app shares location data with third parties and providing a system notification if the app's location-sharing practices change.

entries.title: fgs-types-required.md
entries.summary: This document details changes related to foreground service types in Android 14.  It emphasizes the necessity of declaring appropriate foreground service types in the manifest, the use of ServiceCompat, and the runtime requirements for each type. Several sections were updated for clarity and formatting, including the addition of caution blocks and clarifying the order of operations for permission requests.  New types are also highlighted.

entries.title: partial-photo-video-access.md
entries.summary: This document describes the changes related to Selected Photos Access in Android 14. It outlines how apps can request access to specific images and videos instead of all media, and how to adapt existing gallery pickers to utilize the new READ_MEDIA_VISUAL_USER_SELECTED permission. The changes include updates to permission handling, media re-selection, and querying the device library.

entries.title: schedule-exact-alarms.md
entries.summary: The documentation updates describe changes to the `SCHEDULE_EXACT_ALARM` permission in Android 14.  Specifically, the permission is no longer pre-granted to most new apps targeting Android 13 and higher, and apps must now explicitly request it. This change emphasizes the importance of using exact alarms only when necessary and provides guidance on checking and requesting the permission, as well as alternative approaches for scheduling tasks.

entries.title: app-metadata.md
entries.summary: The documentation for app metadata bundles has been updated to clarify the requirements for data safety information, including how developers should declare data handling practices for apps and third-party SDKs, and the methods for adding the metadata to different app distribution methods.  Formatting and minor wording changes were also made for clarity.

entries.title: grammatical-inflection.md
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to replace the markdown formatting of the 'Note' and 'Caution' sections with the more modern 'Note' and 'Caution' syntax. This change improves the visual clarity and consistency of the document.

entries.title: predictive-back.md
entries.summary: This document describes the implementation of predictive back animations in Android. The changes include improvements in clarifying how to opt-in, use and customize in-app transitions and animations, along with references to the required AndroidX and Material components versions and updated API usages.

entries.title: Android 14 features and changes list  |  Android Developers
entries.summary: This document outlines changes for Android 14.  It covers new features and behavior changes across different areas like accessibility, camera and media, core functionality, user experience, and developer productivity.

entries.title: foreground-service-types.md
entries.summary: This document outlines changes to foreground service types in Android 15. The primary focus is on restrictions for launching foreground services from `BOOT_COMPLETED` broadcast receivers, and includes clarifications on handling of timeouts for `FOREGROUND_SERVICE_MEDIA_PROCESSING` services.

entries.title: setup-sdk.md
entries.summary: This document provides instructions for setting up the Android 15 SDK in Android Studio. The changes include improvements in formatting and wording for better clarity, such as updating the AGP upgrade warning to use a Markdown warning block, and refining the instructions for SDK installation and next steps.

entries.title: summary.md
entries.summary: This document outlines changes for Android 15, categorized by functionality and type, including new features, API updates, behavior changes, and deprecations affecting app developers. The updates cover areas like accessibility, camera and media, connectivity, core functionality, and more.

entries.title: download-ota-qpr.md
entries.summary: The documentation for downloading Android 16 QPR OTA images has been updated to include a warning for internal Google employees and updated device lists that include the Pixel 6a and 8a, and correctly lists Pixel 9 family devices. The update also moves the keywords_public field and rearranges some content for better clarity.

entries.title: progress-centric-notifications.md
entries.summary: This document introduces progress-centric notifications in Android 16. It details the use of `Notification.ProgressStyle` for creating notifications that track user-initiated journeys, emphasizing the use of points and segments to represent milestones and states within a journey, with best practices and example code provided.

entries.title: download-ota.md
entries.summary: The documentation updates the list of supported Pixel devices for OTA images. It also adds a warning for Googlers before sideloading a new release and reorders the device list. These changes reflect the latest supported devices for Android 16 QPR3 updates and provide crucial safety advice.

entries.title: summary.md
entries.summary: This document outlines changes and new features in Android 16 (likely Android 15), covering core functionality, user experience, and system UI. It details behavior changes affecting all apps, apps targeting Android 16, and new APIs.

entries.title: behavior-changes-all.md
entries.summary: Android 17 introduces a limit on the number of keys an app can create in the Android Keystore. Non-system apps targeting Android 17 or higher are limited to 50,000 keys, while all other apps, including system apps, are limited to 200,000 keys. If an app exceeds this limit, key creation will fail with a KeyStoreException, and the getNumericErrorCode() method will return different error codes depending on the target API level. This change prevents apps from creating an excessive number of keys, which could impact the performance of other apps on the device.

entries.title: download-ota.md
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to include the Pixel 6a device and its corresponding OTA download information for Android 17. Additionally, the download section for the OTA system images was duplicated.

entries.title: background.md
entries.summary: This document describes the background execution limits introduced in Android 8.0 (API level 26) to improve user experience by limiting what apps can do while running in the background. It covers background service and broadcast limitations and suggests using JobScheduler for background tasks.

entries.title: android-9.0-changes-all.md
entries.summary: This document outlines behavior changes in Android 9 (API level 28) that affect all apps, regardless of their target API level. These changes primarily focus on improving power management and enhancing user privacy by restricting access to sensors, call logs, phone numbers, and Wi-Fi information. Developers should review these changes and update their apps accordingly to ensure compatibility and adhere to the new privacy guidelines.

entries.title: android-9.0.md
entries.summary: The documentation for Android 9.0 (Pie) has been updated with several changes, primarily involving the replacement of standard markdown notes with the 'NOTE' callout boxes. entries.title: power.md
entries.summary: This document introduces updates to Android 9 (API level 28) regarding power management, focusing on App Standby Buckets and Battery Saver improvements.  The changes detail how the system categorizes apps based on usage and restricts resource access, with guidelines for developers to ensure their apps function efficiently within these new power management strategies.  Key changes include updated descriptions of app standby buckets and actionable notifications.

entries.title: Android Studio v1.5.0 (November 2015)
entries.summary: Android Studio 1.5.0 includes updates to the Memory Monitor for more effective analysis of HPROF files, enabling faster identification of memory leaks. It introduces new deep link and app link support within the code editor, including intent filter creation, integration code generation for the App Indexing API, and deep link testing features. Furthermore, it enhances Vector Asset Studio to support more VectorDrawable elements for backward compatibility and adds new lint checks for Android TV, Android Auto, and improved security checks.

entries.title: Contact picker
entries.summary: This document introduces the Android Contact Picker, a privacy-focused and standardized interface for apps to request contact information from users. The Contact Picker, available on Android 17 (Jelly Bean) and later, provides a secure alternative to requesting the broad `READ_CONTACTS` permission. Instead of accessing the entire address book, apps specify the required data fields (e.g., phone numbers, email addresses), and the user selects which contacts to share. The document explains how to integrate the Contact Picker using `Intent.ACTION_PICK_CONTACTS` and `Intent.EXTRA_REQUESTED_DATA_FIELDS` to specify the desired contact data (using MIME types from `ContactsContract.CommonDataKinds`). It provides Kotlin and Java code examples for launching the picker in both single and multi-selection modes (using `Intent.EXTRA_ALLOW_MULTIPLE` and `Intent.EXTRA_SELECTION_LIMIT`). The document also details how to handle results, including how to query the returned `Session URI` using a `ContentResolver` to access the selected contact data, demonstrating how to process the retrieved data using a `Cursor`. It includes important considerations such as backward compatibility with the legacy `ACTION_PICK` intent, testing on older SDKs (using `EXTRA_USE_SYSTEM_CONTACTS_PICKER`), and best practices: Requesting only necessary data fields; persisting data immediately (as the session URI grants temporary access); and avoiding reliance on account data due to privacy protections.  This documentation aims to guide developers in using the Contact Picker to enhance user privacy and offer a consistent and user-friendly contact sharing experience.

entries.title: FlexBox
entries.summary: This document introduces `FlexBox` in Android Compose, a layout container for arranging items in a single direction (horizontal or vertical). It's designed to resize, wrap, align, and distribute space among items to optimize the use of available space, making it suitable for layouts with varying item sizes and responsiveness to screen size changes. The document emphasizes that `FlexBox` is heavily influenced by the CSS Flexible Box Layout specification, sharing similar concepts like main and cross axes, main and cross sizes, and terminology such as 'grow', 'shrink', and 'basis'. The document explains when to use `FlexBox` (displaying a small number of items within a larger layout, or when wrapping items) and when not to (for large numbers of items, use lazy lists/grids or for overall screen layouts, use `Grid`). It details how to apply properties to the `FlexBox` container (e.g., direction, wrap, justifyContent, alignItems) and individual items (e.g., basis, grow, shrink, alignSelf). Finally, the document outlines the layout algorithm of `FlexBox`, detailing the steps involved in calculating child sizes, sorting items, building lines, and aligning/resizing items on both the main and cross axes. It includes links to other guides for container and item behaviors, and for getting started with `FlexBox`.

entries.title: Set container behavior
entries.summary: This document introduces the configuration options for the `FlexBox` container in Android Compose, a layout element used for arranging items in a flexible manner. It explains how to customize `FlexBox` behavior using a `FlexBoxConfig` block. The primary focus is on the properties within `FlexBoxConfig` that control the layout: `direction`, `alignItems`, `alignContent`, `justifyContent`, `wrap`, `rowGap`, `columnGap`, and `gap`.  The `direction` property sets the main axis (either `Row` or `Column`, with reverse variants), determining the primary direction items are laid out.  `justifyContent` is used to align and distribute items along the main axis, offering options like `Start`, `Center`, `End`, `SpaceBetween`, `SpaceAround`, and `SpaceEvenly`.  `alignItems` controls alignment along the cross axis within a single line, providing options like `Start`, `End`, `Center`, `Stretch`, and `Baseline`.  `alignContent` manages the alignment and spacing of multiple lines (when wrapping is enabled), offering similar options to `justifyContent`.  The `wrap` property enables multi-line layouts; options include `NoWrap`, `Wrap`, and `WrapReverse`.  Finally, the document covers adding gaps between items using `rowGap`, `columnGap`, or the combined `gap` function. The documentation uses visual aids to illustrate the effects of different configuration options, including images showcasing the impact of each property on the item arrangement within the `FlexBox` container. This is a comprehensive guide to utilizing `FlexBox` effectively within Compose layouts.

entries.title: Get started with FlexBox
entries.summary: This document, titled "get-started.md," provides a beginner's guide to implementing basic `FlexBox` layouts in Android Compose. It begins by outlining the necessary steps for setting up the project, which includes adding the `androidx.compose.foundation.layout` library to the project's `lib.versions.toml` and then adding the library dependency to the app's `build.gradle.kts` file. The document then presents two examples to illustrate how `FlexBox` can be used to create layouts. The first example demonstrates a simple `FlexBox` layout with two `Text` elements stacked vertically and center-aligned. The second example showcases a more complex layout, where `FlexBox` wraps five items onto two rows, allowing them to grow unequally to fill the available space with a specified gap between items. Images are included to visualize the resulting layouts. The document concludes by directing readers to further documentation for in-depth information on `FlexBox` behavior, specifically referencing resources on "Set container behavior" and "Set item behavior."

entries.title: Set item behavior
entries.summary:
This document introduces how to control the behavior of individual items within a `FlexBox` layout in Android Compose. It details the use of the `Modifier.flex` modifier to manipulate item size, alignment, and order. The core concepts revolve around three main aspects:

1.  **Item Size:** This section covers how to manage the size of items using `basis`, `grow`, and `shrink` properties.  `basis` defines the initial or preferred size.  Different value types such as `Auto`, fixed `dp`, and percentage are explained, including behavior and example code.  `grow` determines how much an item expands to fill extra space, with examples of how different grow values affect the layout.  `shrink` addresses how items contract when space is insufficient, also detailing its behavior and examples.
2.  **Item Alignment:** This explains how to use `alignSelf` to override the `alignItems` property of the parent `FlexBox` container for specific items. The document describes the possible values for `alignSelf` (including `Auto`) and provides example code, along with a visual representation of the effect of alignment.
3.  **Item Order:** This part describes how to change the visual order of items within the `FlexBox` layout using the `order` property.  It explains that items are laid out in the order they are declared by default and how `order` can be used to reorder them without changing the code's declaration order. Example code shows how items with different order values are displayed, illustrating the impact of `order` on the visual layout.

The document provides code snippets, diagrams, and descriptions to clarify the usage and effect of each property, providing a comprehensive guide to controlling item behavior within a `FlexBox` layout.

entries.title: Productivity  |  Gallery  |  Android Developers
entries.summary:
This document introduces a new page within the Android Developers documentation, specifically focusing on "Productivity" within the "Gallery" section for large screens. It serves as a guide for developers to design Android applications that enhance user productivity on devices with larger displays, such as tablets and foldables. The document's primary purpose is to showcase design patterns and best practices for creating productive and user-friendly experiences on large screens, highlighting features and capabilities not easily achievable on smaller screen devices.

The content is organized into several key sections:

1.  **Introduction:** Sets the context, emphasizing the importance of adapting apps for large screens to enhance productivity and provides links to related documentation.
2.  **Core Concepts and Layouts:** This section describes layout patterns tailored for productivity, including:
    *   **File browsing and filtering:** Displaying thumbnails for easy file navigation.
    *   **Items and overviews:** Showing detailed list information in conjunction with a list.
    *   **Tools and settings (Supporting Pane):** Providing easy access to more tools and settings without obstructing the main content.
    *   **Reviews and comments (Supporting Pane):** Incorporating reviews and comments directly within the document view.
    Each layout pattern includes links to relevant Material Design guidelines and implementation guides.
3.  **Differentiated Experiences:** This section covers features that leverage large-screen capabilities:
    *   **Drag and drop:** Enables intuitive content sharing between different views or apps.
    *   **Tabletop:** Designing layouts suitable for activities like presentations and video conferences.
    *   **Multi-window:** Facilitating multitasking with apps working side-by-side.
    *   **Keyboard, mouse, and trackpad:** Enhancing productivity through input device support.
4.  **Customer Stories:** Presents real-world examples of how large screen features have been implemented to improve user experience, including Chrome and WPS Office, with links to relevant articles.
5.  **Build better with Android:** Provides links to related galleries about shopping and reading.

The "why it matters" is that it helps developers provide enhanced user experiences on large-screen devices, enabling efficient multitasking, intuitive navigation, and overall improved productivity. This, in turn, can lead to increased user engagement and satisfaction, and positions applications for success in a market increasingly focused on devices like tablets and foldables. This document provides practical guidance and promotes best practices for designing and developing Android apps that take full advantage of large-screen capabilities.

entries.title: Reading  |  Gallery  |  Android Developers
entries.summary: This document, titled "Reading" within the "Gallery" section of Android Developers documentation, provides guidance and showcases design considerations for creating optimal reading experiences on large-screen Android devices, particularly tablets and foldables. It emphasizes the advantages large screens offer, such as improved readability and immersive experiences, and suggests ways to leverage these advantages. The document is structured to highlight key features and design patterns for reading applications. The core concepts include "Full-screen readability" via immersive mode to reduce distractions, "Booklists and buys" using multi-column grids and side navigation, and "What's inside" with synopses. It also covers "Complementary content" in supporting panes, "Markup tools," and "News feeds." Furthermore, the document explores "Differentiated experiences" unique to large screens. It highlights multi-window support for multitasking, the ability to mimic a physical book's layout with "Book posture" on foldables, and "Drag and drop" functionality for content manipulation. The document includes links to relevant Android developer documentation for implementation. The document also includes a customer story from Readera LLC emphasizing the positive impact of large-screen adaptation on user reading time. Finally, it links to other areas like "Creativity" and "Games" within the Large Screens Gallery, indicating broader design considerations beyond just reading.

entries.title: Shopping  |  Gallery  |  Android Developers
entries.summary: This document introduces a new page within the Android Developers documentation, specifically focusing on the "Shopping" use case within the "Gallery" section for large screens. It aims to guide developers on designing shopping applications that effectively utilize the increased screen real estate available on larger devices, such as tablets and foldables. The document's primary purpose is to showcase how to enhance the shopping experience on large screens. It details how adaptive apps can present product information, specifications, and related details efficiently. The document outlines core concepts such as "Product specs" (using list-detail layouts), "Catalogs and promotions" (using feed layouts), and "Supporting the sale" (using supporting pane layouts). Each section provides design considerations and links to relevant Material Design guidelines and implementation resources. Furthermore, the document highlights differentiated experiences like "Multi-window" and "Drag and drop," which are not readily available on smaller screens. Finally, the document includes a customer story about eBay, emphasizing how large-screen optimizations led to a higher Google Play rating.  The document concludes with recommendations and links to content about 'Reading' and 'Creativity' user cases.
